\Section{Step-by-Step Example}
\label{sec:example}

\SubSection{Higher-Order Vector Search}

We look at a simple function to find an element in a vector based on a predicate passed as a parameter:

\begin{MoveBox}
  fun find<T>(v: &vector<T>,
              pred: |&T|bool has copy + drop): u64 {
    let i = 0;
    let n = v.length();
    while (i < n) {
      if (pred(&v[i])) return i;
      i = i + 1;
    }
    n
  }
\end{MoveBox}

We now ask Claude Code (\CC) to infer specs for these functions. This will cause it to load a skill which instructs it to create a list of tasks:

\begin{claude}
> infer specs for the functions in this package
● Skill(move-flow:move-inf)
  ⎿ Successfully loaded skill
✽ Zigzagging…
  ⎿ ◻ Synthesize loop invariants
    ◻ Infer weakest preconditions
    ◻ Simplify inferred specs
    ◻ Full-scope verification run
    ◻ Fix logical errors
    ◻ Resolve timeouts
\end{claude}

\noindent The tasks are listed in the suggested execution order but operate on independent snapshots of the package; \CC may interleave them with edits that the user can monitor or override.


\Paragraph{Synthesizing Loop Invariants}
For this task, \CC is instructed to infer loop invariants for loops which do not yet have any.
The skill defines guidelines on how to do this in Move. For the !find! example, this leads to the following code:

\begin{MoveBox}
  public fun find<T>(..)
    ..
    while (i < n) {
      if (pred(vector::borrow(v, i))) return i;
      i = i + 1;
    } spec {
      invariant i <= n;
      invariant n == len(v);
      invariant forall j in 0..i: !result_of<pred>(v[j]);
      invariant forall j in 0..i: !aborts_of<pred>(v[j]);
    };
    n
  }
\end{MoveBox}

\noindent Notice the use of behavioral predicates !result_of! and !aborts_of! to access the specification of another function -- in this case the runtime value !pred! \footnote{Specifications with function values are described in \cite{GrieskampEtAl26HO}}. Behavioral predicates are an important feature for specification inference: we can refer to what other functions are doing, abstracting how they are actually doing it, localizing inference to inter-function analysis.

\Paragraph{Infer weakest preconditions}
For this task, \CC calls \MVP's \WP algorithm via its MCP service. For our example, the following code is produced:

\begin{MoveBox}
  spec find {
    pragma opaque = true;
    ensures
      (forall x: u64: x < len(v)
                      ==> !result_of<pred>(v[x]))
        ==> result == len(v);
    ensures
      forall y: u64: (forall x: u64:
                 x < y ==> !result_of<pred>(v[x]))
        && (y < len(v) && result_of<pred>(v[y]))
        ==> result == y;
    // aborts_if omitted
  }
\end{MoveBox}
%    aborts_if  exists y: u64: (forall x: u64: x < y ==> !result_of<pred>(v[x])) && ((forall x: u64: x < y ==> !aborts_of<pred>(v[x])) && (y < len(v) && aborts_of<pred>(v[y])));
%    aborts_if  exists y: u64: (forall x: u64: x < y ==> !result_of<pred>(v[x])) && ((forall x: u64: x < y ==> !aborts_of<pred>(v[x])) && (y < len(v) && !in_range(v, y)));

\noindent The aborts specification has been omitted since it is verbose. \WP derives semantically correct but not always ideal specifications.

\Paragraph{Simplification of Inferred Specs}
With loop invariants generated by \CC and \WP-derived specs by the Move prover, we now ask \CC again to simplify the resulting specifications.

\begin{MoveBox}
  spec find {
    pragma opaque = true;
    ensures result <= len(v);
    ensures forall j: u64 where j < result:
              !result_of<pred>(v[j]);
    ensures result < len(v)
              ==> result_of<pred>(v[result]);
    aborts_if
       exists j: u64 where j < len(v):
         aborts_of<pred>(v[j])
         && (forall k: u64 where k < j:
               !result_of<pred>(v[k]));
  }
\end{MoveBox}

\noindent This specification has a quite different structure from the one derived with \WP, which served as a baseline for the intended semantics.


\Paragraph{Verification}
The remaining three tasks are verification-driven: a fast full-scope run flushes out logical errors, which are then fixed iteratively, and any timeouts are resolved by introducing lemmas, proof hints, and helper functions (Sec.~\ref{sec:skills}).

\SubSection{Exponentiation}
\label{sec:pow}

We now look at another example, this time in the arithmetic domain.

\begin{MoveBox}
  fun pow(base: u64, exp: u64): u64 {
    let result = 1;
    let i = 0;
    while (i < exp) {
      result = result * base;
      i = i + 1;
    }
    result
  }
\end{MoveBox}

In the first step, \CC derives loop invariants and introduces a recursive helper function to do so:

\begin{MoveBox}
  fun pow(base: u64, exp: u64): u64 {
    ..
    while (i < exp) {
      ..
    } spec {
      invariant i <= exp;
      invariant result == pow_spec(base, i);
    }
    result
  }
  spec fun pow_spec(base: u64, exp: u64): u64 {
    if (exp == 0) 1
    else base * pow_spec(base, exp - 1)
  }
\end{MoveBox}

\WP analysis adds the canonical specification block below.

\begin{MoveBox}
  spec pow {
    ensures result == pow_spec(base, exp);
    aborts_if exp > 0
      && pow_spec(base, exp - 1) * base > MAX_U64;
  }
\end{MoveBox}

 \noindent \CC-based simplification does not find anything to improve here. However, not unexpectedly with non-linear arithmetic such as in this example, verification times out.

Directed by the task instructions for resolving timeouts and using Move's proof hint system, \CC introduces a monotonicity lemma.
This lemma is required to prove the !aborts_if! condition: if at any point earlier in the loop multiplication aborts with overflow, then by monotonicity it also aborts later in the loop.
Note that below, !num! is Move's unbounded precision signed integer type available in specifications:

\begin{MoveBox}
  spec pow {
    .. // function spec above
  } proof {
    // Apply lemma with trigger
    forall x: num, y: num
      {pow_spec(base, x), pow_spec(base, y)}
      apply pow_mono(base, x, y);
  }
  spec lemma pow_mono(base: num, x: num, y: num) {
    requires base >= 1 && 0 <= x && x <= y;
    ensures pow_spec(base, x) <= pow_spec(base, y);
  } proof {
      if (x < y) {
          assert pow_spec(base, y - 1)
                   <= pow_spec(base, y);
          apply pow_mono(base, x, y - 1);
      }
  }
\end{MoveBox}

\SubSection{State-Dependent Inference}

As a final example, we look at a state-dependent problem, where the Move function modifies global storage. This example does not contain loops but illustrates how \WP deals with state.

\begin{MoveBox}
  struct Balance has key { v: u64 }
  fun transfer(from: address, to: address, amount: u64) {
    Balance[from].v -= amount;
    Balance[to].v += amount;
  }
  spec transfer(from: address, to: address, amount: u64) {
    modifies Balance[from];
    modifies Balance[to];
    aborts_if !exists<Balance>(from);
    aborts_if !exists<Balance>(to);
    aborts_if Balance[from].v < amount;
    aborts_if from != to && Balance[to].v + amount > MAX_U64;
    ensures from == to ==> Balance[from] == old(Balance[from]);
    ensures from != to ==> Balance[from].v == old(Balance[from]).v - amount;
    ensures from != to ==> Balance[to].v == old(Balance[to]).v + amount;
  }
\end{MoveBox}

\TODO{}{better example which requires state labels -- for this exampleeven though \WP creates state labels, \CC removes them as they are redundant}
